on Linux, and implement a cloud-native system called Het-
X
r eroK8s based on Kubernetes. We evaluate the systems us-
a  ing NVIDIA Bluefield-2 DPUs and CXL-based DPUs (sim-
ulated with real CXL memory devices). The results show
that HeteroK8s effectively supports complex (unmodified)
commodity cloud-native applications (up to 1 million LoC)
and provides up to 31.9x better latency and 64x less resource
consumption (compared with kernel-bypass design), 60% bet-
ter end-to-end latency, and 55% higher scalability compared
with SOTA systems.

Cloud-native computing has emerged as a prominent para-
digm in cloud and datacenter environments, utilizing con-
tainerization to distribute and deploy applications [3–5, 10,
19]. The fundamental unit of the execution environment,
1
such as a Pod in Kubernetes , typically comprises one or
multiple containers, each containing a standalone image but
sharing a network environment. This approach encapsulates
an application in a portable and self-contained way that can
be deployed and distributed across diverse nodes.
One significant advantage of cloud-native apps compared
to non-cloud-native apps is that they are developed with the
support of extensive cloud infrastructure services, including
storage, network, security, and more. These infrastructures
are also deployed as containers. There are two categories of
infrastructure containers: global infrastructure containers
and per-Pod infrastructure containers, as shown in Figure 1-
a. For instance, the platform scheduler serves as one of the
global infrastructure containers responsible for orchestrat-
ing requests to different Pods, while a sidecar proxy (e.g.,
Envoy [22]) represents a per-Pod infrastructure container
present in each Pod to facilitate the networking functionali-
ties of the application.
While infrastructure containers play a crucial role in de-
livering advantages in cloud-native computing, they also
introduce a non-trivial burden on resources, referred to as
infra-burden in the paper. For example, service mesh [1] has
emerged as a prominent pattern in cloud-native platforms
for managing microservices, employing a dedicated proxy
1
In this paper, we consider the Pod as a generalized representation of the
fundamental computational unit in cloud-native platforms.

Figure 1. Cloud-native platform with infrastructure
containers and user apps.
(known as a sidecar-proxy) to handle network traffic on be-
half of the applications. Since eachmicroservice Pod contains
a sidecar-proxy as an infrastructure container, these proxy
containers can consume up to 25% of the CPU and 33% of
memory resources on a node [66]. The infra-burden chal-
lenge has persisted for a considerable time [54] and presents
substantial difficulty in mitigation [43, 51, 82].
A recent noteworthy trend that could potentially offer
relief is that cloud vendors are increasingly deploying more
heterogeneous devices to enhance scalability, performance,
and cost-efficiency [2, 8, 9, 11, 12, 16, 45, 56, 81]. The Data
Processing Unit (DPU) represents a novel category of het-
erogeneous devices that deliver scalable and energy-efficient
computation capabilities [65], leveraging embedded process-
ing units and an efficient NIC. DPUs have the potential to
significantly enhance instance density in a node (e.g., 50% im-
provement in serverless computing [44]). These devices also
offer enhanced security features through isolated memory
and cores, along with boosted packet processing facilitated
by numerous on-path hardware threads, such as the 256
hardware threads supported in Bluefield-3 [73]. The expec-
tation is that heterogeneous processing units like DPUs will
be interconnected with rapid interconnect technologies like
PCIe 6.0 and CXL [6], providing further performance en-
hancements. Leveraging DPUs could potentially alleviate
the infra-burden challenge.
Goal: Offloading cloud-native infrastructure services
to DPUs. In contrast to the conventional approach of colo-
cating infrastructure containers and user apps on both the
CPU and DPU [18], we propose a promising (albeit challeng-
ing) architecture that involves decoupling these two types
of cloud-native containers onto separate Processing Units
(PUs), as illustrated in Fig.1-b. This strategy can effectively al-
leviate the infra-burden by preventing interference between
2

(cid:12)
Bicheng Yang, Jingkai He, Dong Du  , Yubin Xia, and Haibo Chen
infrastructure containers and app containers. Nevertheless,
separating infrastructure containers from app containers
poses significant challenges.
Challenge-1: Infrastructure and app containers in the
same Pod are coupled with the same network names-
pace, dependent on the single-PU OS. Containers within
a shared Pod utilize a will network namespace provided by
the underlying OS kernel. Consequently, processes in dif-
ferent containers share common network devices and can
communicate across containers using “localhost”. However,
offloading infrastructure containers to DPUs disrupts this
shared network namespace functionality.

Sustaining local
network abstraction while separating infrastructure and app
containers onto different PUs poses a notable challenge.
Challenge-2: Separating containers onto different PUs
has a trade-off between communication performance
and resource utilization. Infrastructure and app contain-
ers will communicate through network. The separation may
significantly increase communication latency (by 10x to
100x) compared to a single-PU design with Linux network
stack as network packets should go through two NICs. Ad-
vanced network solutions such as kernel-bypassed mecha-
nisms [23, 28, 31, 52, 59] like rsocket [28] can provide socket-
level APIs for compatibility and optimal performance to
directly leverage hardware features like RDMA. However,
these solutions often encounter challenges related to subop-
timal resource utilization [89], including memory wastage
from pinned memory allocation for user-space links and
CPU cycle wastage from user-mode polling. Kernel-assisted
approaches like SMC-R [46] have performance drawbacks
due to context switching and data copying overheads. Con-
sequently, a cross-PU and network-based communication
method that can deliver superior performance and maximize
resource utilization is essential but currently unavailable.
Challenge-3: Not all infrastructure containers are suit-
able for offloading to DPUs. The complexity of this chal-
lenge arises from the inherent nature of DPUs, which typ-
ically comprise a wimpy SoC (only) optimized for specific
domain-centric tasks, such as network packet processing. A
simple design for offloading containers to DPUs may carry
significant performance overhead, as suggested by prior
works [44, 54, 84]. As a result, system designers still lack
effective practices to offload cloud-native infrastructure.
This paper introduces HeteroPod, a cross-PU Pod design
that addresses the first two challenges through HeteroNet,
which achieves transparent, high-performant, and resource-
efficient XPU networking. HeteroNet introduces two key
techniques, namely split network namespace (hetero-netns)
and elastic and efficient XPU networking (hetero-socket).
hetero-netns provides a unified network abstraction for
cross-PU containers. The novelty of hetero-netns lies in its
approach to re-do network rules to establish a split network
namespace, enabling direct or indirect communication as.

2.1  Cloud-Native Apps and the Issues
Cloud-native applications are usually deployed in a Pod,
which is a multi-container abstraction in Kubernetes and
other platforms. We classify cloud-native containers into two
types: infrastructure container (infra-container) and user-
container, as shown in Fig.1-a. A promising benefit of cloud-
native apps over non-cloud-native apps is that, cloud-native
apps are developed with the supports of huge cloud infrastruc-
ture services, including storage, network, security, etc. For
example, Envoy [22] is usually deployed as a infra-container
by cloud vendors for each microservice (user-container) to
manage network. We highlight two issues (I) in nowaday
cloud-native systems with such architecture.
I1: Infrastructure containers bring significant burden
on both CPU andmemory. First, infra-containers introduce
non-trivial resource burden. For example, service mesh [1]

Figure 2. Cloud-native apps on CPU-DPU computers.
is a popular pattern to manage microservices with a dedi-
cated proxy, sidecar-proxy, to handle network traffic on the
behalf of the apps (Fig.1-a). We use the emoji-voting [21]
microservice to evaluate the resource costs introduced by
sidecar containers on Kubernetes (detailed settings in §5.1).
Results show that proxy containers can consume up to 25%
of the CPU and 33% of memory resources of a node. Fur-
thermore, proxy containers can consume a lot of resources
even when RPS is low (RPS=20), which exacerbates resource
interference further in a node.
I2: Communication latency becomes more important
for modular cloud-native apps. Microservice and server-
less both are fine-grained and modular apps — an app can
be divided into tens to thousands of microservices or func-
tions. They rely on communication mechanism to cooperate.
As each service only takes few miliseconds for execution,
the communication latency is important for end-to-end per-
formance. In fact, infra-containers like sidecar proxy can
even increase the latency — the latency can increase by 9.5x
(RPS=20)–49.8x (RPS=3K) with proxy (Envoy) [66]. To avoid
the costs, a common practice nowaday is to deploy a function
chain (or highly related microservices) in the same node, and
rely on local communication methods, e.g., IPC or domain
socket, to reduce the latency [34, 44, 53, 58]. This leads to an
important requirement, increasing the density of user apps,
the higer the better. However, because of the interference of
infra-containers, it is hard.
2.2  CPU-DPU Platforms for Cloud-Native Apps
DPUs (data processing units) [14, 68, 70] are a new class of
programmable devices that move and process data around
the data centers. It is an SoC and capable of running commer-
cial OSes and performance-critical programs like network
proxy. DPU usually includes multi-core processing unit (e.g.,
2.75GHz ARM cores in Bluefield-2 DPU) and efficient net-
work interface. Prior works have shown that DPU can im-
prove the density of serverless functions [44], reduce the
latency and resource costs for DFS [54], and enable energy-
efficient microservices [65].
Efforts using DPU for cloud-native apps. Commercial
cloud platforms commonly use either pod-level distribution,
e.g., NVIDIADPUContainer [27] and RedHat OpenShift [18],
or static split [54, 62, 78], e.g., Azure [45], as shown in Fig.2-
a/b. Pod-level distribution treats DPUs as individual servers

The core idea of HeteroPod is simple: selectively deploy in-
frastructure and app containers to DPUs, keeping others on
CPUs. This container-granular deployment utilizes each con-
tainer’s self-contained nature to simplify decoupling. But the
key challenge is cross-container communication, which
depends on network interactions in cloud-native platforms.
Specifically, HeteroPod must address two technical chal-
lenges: (1) enabling transparent cross-PU networking to sup-
port unmodified applications (Challenge-1 in §1). (2) achiev-
ing high-efficiency cross-PU communication (Challenge-2
in §1). To overcome these challenges, we design HeteroNet

Figure 3. Two types of network calls and challenges of
split network namespace.
as the foundational technology for HeteroPod. HeteroNet
introduces two key innovations for cross-PU (XPU) network-
ing.
First, HeteroNet implements hetero-netns, a unified net-
work abstraction for containers across PUs. hetero-netns
utilizes the global port space as an indicator to support
cross-PU direct connection, and leverages the observation
that network filters/rules can be applied redundantly in
both source and destination PUs (as long as they do not
introduce new side effects) to enable cross-PU indirect com-
munication. As a result, hetero-netns creates a split net-
work namespace, preserving communication semantics be-
tween components across PUs. Second, HeteroNet intro-
duces hetero-socket, a kernel-library co-designed network
stack that simultaneously achieves kernel-bypass perfor-
mance, kernel-assisted resource efficiency, and socket API
compatibility. hetero-socket employs two keymechanisms:
speculative allocation is a two-stage buffer management strat-
egy where processes temporarily claim buffers from shared
pools (kernel-bypass manner), with the kernel committing
allocations post-validation, and user-mode NAPI leverages
cross-PU OS coordination to enable interrupt-driven pro-
cessing in user-mode libraries, significantly reducing polling
overhead.

4.1  Split Network Namespace (hetero-netns)
Notation. Network namespaces in Linux [25] provide isola-
tion of network-related system resources, including network
devices, protocol stacks, IP routing tables, port numbers and
so on. We extend the concept to split network namespace,

HeteroPod: XPU-Accelerated Infrastructure Offloading for Commodity Cloud
which provides a unified and isolated network abstraction
in a CPU-DPU computer. Split network namespace can be
exclusively used by one container (multiple processes) or
can be shared by multiple containers (e.g., Kubernetes Pod).
Network calls. We can classify network requirements into
two types (Fig.3-a/b/c): direct call and indirect call. Direct call
indicates two processes within the network namespace can
communicate by using localhost and port number. Indirect
call refers to the scenario where a message is not directly
transmitted to a local receiver, instead, it is initially intended
for a remote receiver but is then forwarded to a local receiver
due to packet filtering rules, such as iptables or routing.
Challenges. First, challenges arise when multiple kernels
manage processes within the “same” network namespace.
For direct call, the sender’s OS kernel faces the difficulty
of determining which kernel is responsible for the receiver’s
processes. Naively, the OS kernel on the sender side could
forward packets to the kernel on the receiver side, but the is-
sue lies in the lack of information regarding which processes
are deployed on which PUs, as shown in Fig.3-d.
Second, indirect call further introduces a new challenge
called state synchronization, where OS kernels on different
PUs may explicitly synchronize certain states to process the
packets accurately. For example (Fig.3-e), a sidecar proxy
container is deployed to intercept incoming and outgoing
packets from application containers. Typically, this is accom-
plished by adding iptables rules to the network namespace
of the Pod, redirecting packets sent by the application to
the port where the proxy accepts them. These iptables rules
modify the destination IP and port information to match that
of the proxy. The OS kernels store the original destination
IP/port information for future reference (Fig. 3-e, step-2/3).
Upon receiving the packet, the proxy container applies filter-
ing processes and utilizes the getsockopt() syscall to retrieve
the original destination IP/port information (Fig. 3-e, step-
4/5). A naive solution would be to add a similar rule on the
sender side (i.e., the application container) to modify the
destination IP and port, changing them to match the desired
receiver’s PU. However, this poses a challenge because the
proxy container (located on another PU) cannot retrieve the
original IP and port information. The sender’s OS kernel
(CPU in the fig), rather than the receiver’s (DPU in the fig),
stores this crucial data. Although it is possible to synchronize
the data between two PUs, it is very hard in practice because
of the complicated rules and states.
4.1.1  Port-based Reverse Mapping We have observed
that in a split network namespace, processes distributed
across different PUs still share the same logical port space.
Each port number can only be exclusively used by one pro-
cess. Building on this observation, we propose the design
of port-based reverse mapping. This approach involves main-
taining a reverse mapping table that keeps track of the port
5

hetero-socket

IP  IP  IP  IP
Driver  Driver  Driver  Driver

(a) Linux socket  (b) hetero-socket w/ hetero-netns
Figure 4. Communication path with infra-containers.
numbers and their corresponding PUs. The mapping is glob-
ally shared by all HeteroNet daemons on each PU. When a
container needs to use a port number, the HeteroNet daemon
checks the table to ensure that the port number is available.
If the port is already in use, the operation will fail.
To avoid the overhead of port synchronization and check-
ing during the data plane, HeteroNet adopts a plane decou-
pling approach. When there are changes in port information,
the HeteroNet daemon generates and applies a set of single-
PU packet filtering rules, primarily utilizing iptables and
routing in the prototype. These rules automatically detect
the remote port number and employ DNAT (destination NAT)
and SNAT (source NAT) techniques to correctly forward the
packet to the appropriate PUs. As a result, both the sender
and receiver processes remain unaware of these underlying
changes and can continue communicating using localhost
with (almost) zero cost as if nothing has changed.
4.1.2  Partially Idempotent Packet Rules To overcome
the second challenges, we propose partially idempotent packet
rules.

Specifically, instead of performing the packet rules only
in the source PU and synchronize the states latter (which is
hard), we transfer the original rules into two sets of new rules
that will be applied to source and destination PUs. These
rules may have redundant operations to a packet, but not in-
troduce new side effects (i.e., idempotent in this case). States
can be synchronized automatically by re-doing operations
in both PUs.
For example, in the case of Fig.3-e, instead of simply chang-
ing the dst to receiver’s IP/port and saving the orig-dst in the
sender’s PU, we split it into three rules (simplified): (1, on
sender’s PU) routing the pkt from sender’s PU to receiver’s
PU which will not change the pkt, (2, on receiver’s PU) rout-
ing the pkt to the receiver’s container, and (3, on receiver’s
PU) changing the dst and save the orig-dst. These rules per-
form redundant operations — (1/2) and (3) both moving the
pkt to the receiver PU, however, it will not change the be-
havior but achieve states synchronized. As indirect call is
achieved through network rules, hetero-netns can detect
and replace them with the new rules for interception about
incoming and outgoing traffic.

4.2  Elastic and Efficient XPU Networking
4.2.1  Overview Traditional network stack introduces un-
ncessary redundancy for XPU communication which causes
longer latency and restricts the optimization opportunities of
fast interconnect. For example, in microservice with sidecar,
a message has to go through the network stack three times
to arrive at its destination, as shown in Fig. 4. Although there
are kernel-bypass socket-compatible systems [7, 23, 31], they
suffer poor resource utilization because of pinned memory
buffer and static allocation (§4.2.2). hetero-socket aims to
achieve the same performance as prior kernel-bypass design,
but overcoming the resource challenges in the XPU setting.
Cross-PU interconnects as global shared memory. We
summarize one key primitive for XPU communication, global
shared memory (or GShm), which means there would be a
shared memory that can be used as a channel for cross-PU
communication. This assumption already holds for existing
DPUs [15, 68], e.g., Bluefield-2 supports RDMA to allowCPU
and DPU to share an explictly allocated region. To be gen-
eral to different interconnects, hetero-socket introduces
an IAL (interconnect abstraction layer), which abstracts the
interconnect details but only expose GShm abstractions to
the core of hetero-socket.
Decoupling of control and data paths. hetero-socket
relies on Linux network stack to establish a connection (with
hetero-netns), and will form a GShm-based channel for
the best performance when available. During runtime, the
data-path APIs like read() and write() will be intercept us-
ing GShm when possible, but apps can still utilize control
path operations like getsockopt() to get the metadata for a
connection. When containers are deployed on different PUs,
hetero-socket relies on sockets and hetero-netns to es-
tablish a correct connection and then utilizes the GShm to
boost communication.
4.2.2  Challenges Although kernel-bypassed and shared
memory-based design is quite common can achieve best per-
formance by fully utilizing hardware capabilities, HeteroK8s
aims to further resolve two technical challenges which are
not resolved yet. First (Pinned resource costs), the alloca-
tion of shared memory regions is usually privileged opera-
tions that require the OSes of two PUs to negotiate using
interconnect primitives. This procedure is usually slow (e.g.,
RDMA [85]), making dynamically extend the regions hard.
As a result, existing systems tend to reserve a fixed size of
region in OS for a connection to use, facing the trade-off
between resource wastes and performance slowdown. The
issue is significant in real-world as there may be thousands
of connections in a machine. Second (Polling costs), as OS
kernel is bypassed, user-mode stacks are responsible to ex-
plictly poll the data, leading to an old trade-off between PU
costs and long latency.

4.2.3  User-mode Resource Management with Specu-
lative Allocation hetero-socket employs an OS-library
co-design for efficient memory management (Fig. 5). The
core abstraction is the record — a page-aligned buffer with
metadata (e.g., nextRecord), unidirectionally accessible by
either sender or receiver processes.

Each connection main-
tains two local records (one per direction) and leverages
per-core shared arenas containing dynamically allocatable
records, managed as a list with headRecord and tailRecord.
This hybrid approach balances performance (local records)
and scalability (shared arenas) while addressing three key
challenges:
Isolation & synchronization. Shared arenas require strict
access control: processes may only access their connection’s
records or their core’s free records. hetero-socket enforces
this via memory mapping – during scheduling, the OS maps
(1) local records, (2) occupied shared records for active con-
nections, and (3) core-local free records. Per-core arenas
eliminate synchronization overhead as free records are ex-
clusively accessible during a process’s time slice.
Dynamic record chaining. Records form linked lists via a
nextRecord metadata. When a sender exhausts buffer space, it
speculatively appends free records from its core’s arena with-
out kernel involvement (Fig. 5-b). The kernel later commits
these allocations during context switches or exceptions (e.g.,
page faults) by remapping records as occupied (Fig. 5c-d).
This two-phase approach — user-space speculation followed
by kernel ratification—- achieves kernel-bypass performance
with 64× less pinned memory (§6.1). For the receiver process
of a connection, it may access a record that is newly allocated
by the sender, which will trigger a page fault because of lack-
ing permissions to access the free records. In this case, the
OS kernel will handle the page fault by mapping all newly
allocated records to the receiver (also finishing the commit).
This trapping is rare in practice.
Resource governance. Rate-limiting policies prevent resource
hoarding by bounding record usage per process. The ker-
nel enforces these during commit phases while preserving
core-local allocation advantages..

HeteroPod: XPU-Accelerated Infrastructure Offloading for Commodity Cloud
Design summary. As a result, hetero-socket can achieve
similar resource management effectiveness as kernel-centric
design except changing the gloabl resource pool to per-core
pools (shared arena). This change makes sense to avoid
cross-core interferences and synchronization. Compared
with kernel-bypass network design with pre-reserved mem-
ory, hetero-socket achieves similar performance as the
trapping on the receiver side is very rare. In fact, we even
observe performance improvement as the dynamic resource
of hetero-socket can better dispatch resources among con-
nections and better suit for varying loads.
4.2.4  User-modeNAPI hetero-socket introduces the U-
NAPI, a user-space adaptation of Linux’s NAPI [32] that elim-
inates polling overhead through interrupt-driven event han-
dling. Traditional kernel-bypass designs waste CPU cycles
polling for events — U-NAPI instead dynamically switches
between interrupt and polling modes based on traffic pat-
terns, mirroring NAPI’s adaptive behavior in OS kernels.
Event management. hetero-socket keeps standard epoll
semantics and adds two key syscalls: enable_notify_gshm
and disable_notify_gshm. These allow applications to regis-
ter interest in events (e.g., buffer availability) with the kernel.
enable_notify_gshm supports two modes (using different
flags): sync and async. The sync flag blocks threads until
events occur (ideal for blocking I/O), while async triggers
signal handlers that disable interrupts and enable polling
for latency-sensitive phases. The key innovation here is that
U-NAPI leverages OS kernel to simulate the hardware to
provide interrupt capability for U-mode event management.
Integration with network operations. In practice, U-NAPI
optimizes three common scenarios. First, a network write (or
send) operation may block and turns to polling because the
buffer of a connection is full. In this case, the hetero-socket
uses enable_notify_gshm(token, sync|W_flag), which blocks
in the kernel until the connection is ready for write. Sec-
ond, a similar case is blocking read (or recv) on an empty
connection. The hetero-socket can use sync mode with
R_flag. Last, to handle the case to add a new event to an
epoll instance, hetero-socket can use async mode to mon-
itor events, i.e., enable_notify_gshm(token, async|events).
Then it can use the signal handler to handle any event up-
dates, disabling notification, and turn to polling for perfor-
mance.
Cross-PU event propagation. U-NAPI uses two cross-PU
event delivery techniques. First, proactive notification has the
remote side of a connection proactively tell the remote (or
same PU) kernel of a new event. E.g., in blocking write, when
the remote receiver reads data, checks status, and finds the
sender blocked, it uses a syscall to tell the kernel the connec-
tion status changed, which eventually alerts the sender space
may be available. Second, Notify-on-RW (NoRW) makes the
kernel tell the remote (or same) kernel to revoke connection
7

-Native Applications
record permissions (e.g., no read-permission in the write
case). This causes page faults on the receiver’s read, which
are sent to the sender’s kernel to notify of the connection
status change. Though cross-PU latency exceeds local cases,
apps retain the flexibility to revert to polling for critical paths.
4.3  Implementation
hetero-netns. hetero-netns runs as a daemon process on
each PU (Linux) to monitor local networking rules and co-
ordinate with other PUs within the same computer. It main-
tains ports and intercepts network rules within a network
namespace. hetero-netns leverages existing networking
techniques, e.g., nsenter, NAT, policy routing, and iptables. In
HeteroK8s, we extend Kubernetes’ CNI with hetero-netns.
hetero-socket. hetero-socket is written in C/C++. We
leverage LD_PRELOAD [13] to replace socket-related func-
tions (implemented in libc) of an application at runtime.
Besides, we implement global shared memory (GShm) based
on CXL (on CXL-DPU) and RDMA (on Bluefield-2). The
hetero-socket implements a full set of Linux socket inter-
faces. We extend Linux kernel to support the speculative
allocation and the interfaces of U-NAPI.
Multi-ISA supports. We use the multi-platform container
image feature [26] to support multi-ISA PUs in the same
computer, which is a standard feature in Docker/Kubernetes.
Security. HeteroNet is implemented as a per-process library
now, which will maintain the global sharedmemory between
two entites in userspace. As each process can only access the
shared memory for its own connections, HeteroNet will not
introduce new security flaws to attack other processes. Ama-
licious process may break the connection, but can not cause
other issues like overflow because of careful pointer opera-
tions.

Moreover, HeteroNet allows administrator to configure
which connections are allowed to utilize the hetero-socket
by a configuration file, which is similiar like firmware to ex-
plictly specify the allowed list.
Experimental setup. We use two settings for evaluation.
First (Bluefield-DPU), we use two servers, each with 2xE5-
2650 v5 CPU (12cores, 2.2GHz each CPU), 96GB DDR4-1600
memory, and one Nvidia Bluefield-2 DPU devices. Each
Bluefield-2 DPU has 8 ARM Cortex-A72 cores (2.75GHz),
16GB DDR-1600 DRAM and ConnectX-6 (2x 100Gbps RDMA
ports) NIC.We also validate the results on Bluefield-3. Second
(CXL-DPU), we use a dual-socket server with Intel Xeon
Platinum 8468V (2.9 GHz, 48 cores) and a 32GB 4800MT/s
DDR5 RDIMM is connected through a CXL memory expan-
sion card (CXL 2.x). We implement CXL-based DPU with
two VMs (representing CPU and DPU) and utilize the real
CXL device (configured as dax dev) as the GShm.

Reservation. We present end-to-end latency. The unit is milliseco
5  HeteroK8s: XPU Cloud-Native System
We present HeteroK8s, a cloud-native system based on Ku-
bernetes that utilize HeteroNet to effectively and efficiently
utilize DPUs under different scenarios. We present three case
studies, offloadingworker node’s infra-apps (§5.1), offloading
apps (§5.2), and offloading control plane’s infra-apps (§5.3).
5.1  Case Study: Service Mesh and Microservice
Service mesh is a representative pattern which injects a
sidecar container (proxy) into each microservice Pod, leading
to both resource and communication latency challenges. We
illustrate how HeteroK8s can utilize HeteroNet to optimize
the costs caused by proxy-related infra-containers.
Baseline systems with Istio. Besides the basic proxy de-
sign (Fig.6-a), Istio [24] (SOTA mesh system) supports an
advanced architecture, waypoint sidecar [17] (Fig.6-b), that
can effectively mitigate costs. Specifically, it decouples the
sidecar into a zero-trusted tunnel (ztunnel) and a waypoint
sidecar. Ztunnel can be shared by all apps on a node for L4
policies, while a waypoint sidecar is dedicated to an app
(for L7 policies) and can be scheduled to other nodes. The
decoupling can save resources on the apps’ node, however,
it also increases the latency for the normal path resulting
from the scheduling of waypoint sidecars on a third server.
HeteroK8s-Mesh. We design HeteroK8s-Mesh to balance
performance and scalability with efficient offloading of side-
cars to DPUs with HeteroNet. HeteroK8s-Mesh supports

two deployment methods: (1) offloading all proxy-related
infra-containers (ztunnel and waypoint) to DPU (Fig.6-d),
so that CPU can focus on running microservice apps, and
(2) offloading waypoint containers to DPU (Fig.6-c). It will
launch new ztunnel and waypoint containers to CPU when
DPU devices fail or their resources are insufficient to ensure
the availability and QoS of microservices.

If the client and
server microservices are on the same node, HeteroK8s-Mesh
will deploy the ztunnel and waypoint containers in the CPU
when possible. HeteroK8s-Mesh will utilize hetero-netns
to support indrect communication between apps and proxy,
and rely on hetero-socket to speed up the communication
latency.
Methodology. We deploy DeathStarBench [48] and Function-
Bench [55] as microservices, and deploy each microservice
with an Istio waypoint (using Envoy) as gateway to evalu-
ate the performance of HeteroK8s-Mesh. All incoming and
outgoing traffic of microservices will be intercepted and pro-
cessed by the ztunnel on the node. We compare HeteroK8s-
Mesh with two systems: IstioCPU and IstioBaselineDPU.
IstioCPU will only deploy microservice Pods on the CPU,
which represents the best performance in many cases, but
can not effectively utilize DPUs to improve the scalability.
IstioBaselineDPU is implemented based on Istio to deploy
microservice Pods on both CPU and DPU (priorly scheduled
to DPU). It represents existing approaches utilizing DPU
using pod granularity.

HeteroPod: XPU-Accelerated Infrastructure Offloading for Commodity Cloud
End-to-end performance. We evaluate microservices’ end-
to-end latency under two baseline systems, HeteroK8s-WP
(Fig. 6-c) and HeteroK8s-All (Fig. 6-d). To show dynamic
split’s benefits, HeteroK8s-Mesh does not use hetero-socket
here.
The evaluation results for FunctionBench are shown in
Fig.7-a. Compared with the IstioBaselineDPU, HeteroK8s-
Mesh can shorten microservices’ end-to-end latency up to
60% (dd, pyaes, chameleon). The performance of all apps is
improved after deploying with HeteroK8s-Mesh. Compared
to only using CPU, using HeteroK8s-Mesh-WP to offload
waypoint to DPU only brings end-to-end latency overhead
of less than 5%. Using HeteroK8s-Mesh-All to offload ztunnel
and waypoint to DPU can saves more CPU resources, and
only brings end-to-end overhead of 1%-2%(dd, pyaes) and
<1% for all other apps. The reason why the end-to-end la-
tency overhead of using HeteroK8s-Mesh-All is smaller than
that of HeteroK8s-Mesh-WP is that after the service mesh
infrastructure is fully offloaded to DPU, the microservices
running on the CPU are subject to less competition for CPU
and microarchitecture resources (such as cache and TLB),
which helps to shorten the running time of microservices.
The evaluation results for DeathStar are shown in Fig. 7-b.
We do not offload ztunnel here as microservices in DeathStar
have short runtime latency. HeteroK8s-Mesh can achieve
29%–74% better latency compared with BaselineDPU, and
it incurs only <1.2ms latency costs (on average) compared
with CPU (the ideal performance without offloading).
Scalability. We compare the scalability under high density.
Specifically, both HeteroK8s-Mesh and IstioBaselineDPU
will use all DPU and most CPU resources to deploy Pods.
Each microservice pod is paired with a waypoint proxy. The
total number of microservices and waypoints is the same
(70 instances), and the same resources (CPU and memory)
are allocated, i.e., 128MB memory and 100 mCPU per way-
point, and 256MB memory and 300 mCPU per app. We use
Pyaes as the testcase. Requests are evenly load-balanced to
all microservices in a random manner using Nginx.
The average latency results are presented in Fig.7-c/d and
the P99 latency in Fig.7-e/f. Under different RPS, microser-
vices deployed using HeteroK8s-Mesh achieve better perfor-
mance than the IstioBaselineDPU — 39% improved end-to-
end latency. When the RPS rises, the latency of microservices
deployed using the baseline scheme increases rapidly, while
the latency using the HeteroK8s-Mesh scheme can remain
relatively stable. This means that HeteroK8s-Mesh has better
scalability than baseline.
Performance breakdown. We present a breakdown using
large and small apps. We break the end-to-end latency into
five parts: in-node (including inter-PU and cross-PU) com-
munication latency, cross-node communication latency, and
processing latency of ztunnel, waypoint and application. As
shown in Fig.8, for large app, HeteroK8s-Mesh (hetero-netns)

and achieve even better performance compared with Isti-

5.2  Case Study: Serverless Computing (FaaS)
Recent works [42, 44, 77, 86] have tried to explore hetero-
geneous devices (e.g., DPU) for serverless to achieve better
scalability and performance, e.g., Molecule [44]. However,
they usually require non-trivial modifications to both the
serverless runtime and applications.
HeteroK8s-FaaS. HeteroK8s supports serverless computing
on CPU-DPU computers without modifying binaries of func-
tions, language runtimes, and serverless runtime (Fig.6-e).
Unlike Molecule which utilizes one serverless runtime to
manage both CPU and DPU, HeteroK8s-FaaS takes different
PUs as standalone devices, but incorporating the HeteroNet
to achieve efficient communication between functions trans-
parently. As Molecule’s function are single-container sand-
boxes, HeteroK8s-FaaS does not apply the hetero-netns.
We implement HeteroK8s-FaaS based on the Molecule’s
open-sourced homogeneous implementation, and utilizes
its modified runc which support container fork for low la-
tency startup. HeteroK8s-FaaS will preload HeteroNet for
each function instance, but configure a configuration file to
only enable hetero-socket between instances in the same
function chain, i.e., one-function applications will not utilize
HeteroNet. HeteroK8s-FaaS assumes the global gateway will
schedule a function chain in a one heterogeneous computers.
We compare HeteroK8s-FaaS with Molecule and Molecule-
homo (a homogeneous version for CPU-only computer).
Microbenchmarks. We perform microbenchmarks to ana-
lyze the communication latency between two functions in a
chain. We take Alexa skills [88] as an example, and compare
HeteroK8s-FaaS with Molecule (using nIPC) and the baseline
(Molecule’s homogeneous version using Node.js Express)..

6.1  hetero-socket Performance
We conduct an evaluation of network performance with
hetero-socket.We utilize two test cases, lat_tcp and bw_tcp
fromLMBench [67], to evaluate network latency and through-
put. We compare three systems: (1) Baseline is the network
performance in a single PU using Linux network, (2) Reserve
is a version of HeteroNet that reserves a specific GShm for
connection, e.g., 1MB means the connection will reserve
two 1MB buffers for use (two directions), and (3) HeteroNet
(128KB for local records and 128MB for shared arena).

System supports for heterogeneous computers. There are
many related research efforts [33, 35–37, 40, 41, 47, 49, 56,
64, 72, 74, 76, 78–81, 83]. Distributed OSes [36, 37, 80] are
proposed to run a single OS (with multi-kernels) to manage
heterogeneous devices. Some systems use smart devices to

This paper presents HeteroPod with HeteroNet, a novel de-
sign that enables dynamic split of cloud-native apps on CPU-
DPU computers. HeteroNet includes hetero-netns for uni-
fied network namespace and hetero-socket to achieve both
kernel-bypass performance and kernel-assisted resource-
efficiency. We build HeteroK8s to show the benefits on rep-
resentative cloud-native scenarios including serverless com-
puting and microservices. HeteroNet will be open-sourced.

HeteroPod: XPU-Accelerated Infrastructure Offloading for Commodity Cloud
